findings.title: Worker pool shared `idx` counter: OOB access possible when last slot races
findings.description: runWorkerPool increments a shared `idx` counter (`const myIdx = idx++`) across CONCURRENCY concurrent async workers. In JavaScript the post-increment on a Number is not interruptible mid-operation, so two workers cannot receive the same myIdx. However, TWO workers can both pass the `while (idx < stocks.length)` guard before either increments: worker A reads `idx === N-1`, passes the while-check; context switch; worker B reads `idx === N-1`, passes the while-check; both do `idx++`, one gets myIdx=N-1 and the other gets myIdx=N. Worker B then accesses `stocks[N]` which is `undefined` (past the end of the array). The `if (!stock) continue` guard on line 1969 catches this, but the access itself is technically out-of-bounds. More importantly: this guard is the ONLY defence — if it is ever removed, `processOne(undefined)` would throw a TypeError classified as mapper-bug, triggering `process.exit(1)`.
findings.mechanism: Two workers both satisfy `idx < stocks.length` before either increments. After the increment one worker holds myIdx = stocks.length (out of bounds). The while-guard is checked on the shared `idx` value, not `myIdx`, so the OOB slot is claimed before detection.
findings.reproducer: CONCURRENCY >= 2, final two tickers processed in parallel. Both workers pass `while (idx < stocks.length)` simultaneously. One claims myIdx=last valid, other claims myIdx=length.
findings.impact: In current code: silently skipped via `if (!stock) continue`. If guard is removed or bypassed: TypeError in processOne → errClass=mapper-bug → process.exit(1). No current data corruption.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a belt-and-suspenders bounds check immediately after the increment: `const myIdx = idx++; if (myIdx >= stocks.length) break;` This replaces the reactive `if (!stock) continue` with a proactive guard that also exits the loop cleanly.

findings.title: FTS cache write for the same ticker can race between workers; failure silently swallowed
findings.description: When the FTS cache is absent or stale for a ticker, each worker fetches FundamentalsTimeSeries and writes the result to `fundamentals-cache/<ticker>.json` via `writeFileAtomic`. If the watchlist contains duplicate ticker entries (possible after merge from multiple discovery sources without deduplication), two workers can process the same ticker simultaneously. Both will independently fetch FTS and both will write to the same cache path. On Windows (where development occurs), the second `renameSync` can fail with EPERM/EBUSY if the first rename's file handle is briefly held by the OneDrive sync client. The exception at line 1706 is caught by a bare `catch (e) {}` — the write failure is completely silent. If the write fails for any other reason (disk full, permissions), there is no log entry, no failure counter increment, and the ticker proceeds to use the just-fetched (in-memory) FTS data for this run but loses the cache for subsequent runs.
findings.mechanism: Bare `catch (e) {}` at line 1706 swallows all FTS cache write errors. Duplicate ticker in watchlist triggers concurrent FTS fetch and cache write for the same file.
findings.reproducer: Duplicate ticker in watchlist.json (possible after refresh-universe.js merge) + CONCURRENCY >= 2. Or: disk-full condition on any write.
findings.impact: Silent cache write failure. Ticker re-fetches FTS on every subsequent run, consuming extra rate-limit budget. If the failure is persistent (permissions), the 28-day cache is never populated and every run does a fresh FTS fetch for affected tickers.
findings.suggested_fix: Replace `catch (e) {}` at line 1706 with `catch (e) { _log('WARN', 'FTS cache write failed for ' + stock.ticker + ': ' + e.message); }`. Separately, deduplicate watchlist.stocks at the start of pullAll.

findings.title: FTS fetch has no retry; 30s outer timeout covers 4 sequential calls (~5s each = tight budget)
findings.description: The FTS call is wrapped in a single `_withTimeout(fetchFundamentalsTS(...), 30000, ...)` with no retry loop. `fetchFundamentalsTS` issues 4 sequential awaits (annualFin, quarterlyFin, annualCash, annualBs). Each sub-call is individually try/caught, but the OUTER 30s timer starts before the first sub-call. With 4 sequential HTTP calls averaging 5-8s each on slow Yahoo responses, the 30s budget can expire before all 4 finish. When the outer timeout fires, the exception propagates to `processOne`'s catch block and is classified as `errClass='timeout'` — the ENTIRE ticker fails. This contrasts with `quoteSummaryWithRetry` which gets 3 retry attempts with 15s/45s/90s backoff for both rate-limit and timeout errors. FTS errors get zero retries and a tighter time budget.
findings.mechanism: 30s outer timeout covers 4 sequential HTTP calls. Average FTS call time 5-8s × 4 = 20-32s — within budget only if all 4 calls finish on time. One slow FTS endpoint (annualBs is particularly slow for international tickers) causes the whole ticker to fail.
findings.reproducer: EM-region ticker where Yahoo FTS balance-sheet endpoint has 8+ second latency. Total FTS time exceeds 30s. errClass=timeout in failures array.
findings.impact: Ticker fails entirely despite quoteSummary having succeeded. Over a 15k-ticker universe this can produce hundreds of false-timeout failures per run, masking the true rate-limit failure signal.
findings.suggested_fix: Increase FTS timeout from 30s to 60s. Add a retry wrapper for FTS analogous to quoteSummaryWithRetry (retry on timeout or 429). At minimum, if FTS times out but the canonical object is already populated from quoteSummary, write the partial snapshot instead of discarding it.

findings.title: annualRev FTS override uses array length comparison — null-filled QS arrays block FTS override
findings.description: `if (ftsAnnual.annualRev.length > canonical.annual.annualRev.length)` uses length comparison to decide whether to override quoteSummary-derived annualRev with FTS data. Since mapFTSToAnnual pushes null placeholders for empty rows (the F-DP-030/031 fix), both FTS and QS arrays can have the same length (e.g., 4 entries) while the QS array contains all nulls (post-Nov-2024 schema drift) and the FTS array contains valid revenue values. When lengths are equal, the condition is false, and the null-filled QS annualRev remains in the canonical snapshot. The non-null count comparison applied to annualOpInc (lines 1717-1729) was never applied to annualRev.
findings.mechanism: `mapYahooToCanonical` reads `incomeStatementHistory` which returns 4 null-revenue rows post-Nov-2024. `mapFTSToAnnual` also produces 4 rows (null-placeholder for the empty row, non-null for the populated rows). Length comparison `4 > 4 = false`. FTS override does not fire.
findings.reproducer: Non-US ticker where Yahoo quoteSummary incomeStatementHistory returns sparse/null revenue since Nov 2024 but FTS annualFin has valid revenue for the same years.
findings.impact: annualRev stays null-filled in the snapshot. Every downstream method dependent on annualRev (FCF yield, P/S, ROIC, reinvestment-rate, Rule-of-40, EV/EBITDA) returns computable:false. Silent data absence for a large fraction of the international universe.
findings.suggested_fix: Replace line 1710 with non-null count comparison: `const _qsRevNonNull = (canonical.annual.annualRev||[]).filter(v=>v!=null&&v.value!=null).length; const _ftsRevNonNull = (ftsAnnual.annualRev||[]).filter(v=>v!=null&&v.value!=null).length; if (_ftsRevNonNull > _qsRevNonNull) canonical.annual.annualRev = ftsAnnual.annualRev;`

findings.title: annualNetIncome FTS override uses array length — same null-density bug as annualRev
findings.description: `if (ftsAnnual.annualNetIncome.length > canonical.annual.annualNetIncome.length)` uses a length comparison identical to the annualRev bug (F-DP-004). mapFTSToAnnual pushes null placeholders for rows that have at least one non-null field across all four income columns. Both QS and FTS can have length-4 arrays where QS has all nulls (post-Nov-2024 schema drift on incomeStatementHistory.netIncome) and FTS has valid values. The condition `4 > 4 = false`, so the null-filled QS netIncome array is never overridden.
findings.mechanism: Same root cause as F-DP-004: length guard ignores null density.
findings.reproducer: Non-US ticker where Yahoo quoteSummary incomeStatementHistory.netIncome is null for all 4 years (schema drift) but FTS annualFin has valid netIncome.
findings.impact: annualNetIncome stays null-filled. Methods depending on netIncome (ni-volatility-guard, loss-magnitude-guard, Altman Z-score, ROE, 8-quarter earnings-stability) return computable:false.
findings.suggested_fix: Replace line 1766 with non-null count comparison matching the opInc pattern at lines 1717-1729.

findings.title: Post-FTS opInc fallback passes empty `[]` as isHist — bank/insurance derivation paths unreachable
findings.description: The post-FTS sector-aware OpInc fallback calls `_deriveOpIncForFinancials([], _postRev, _opMargFrac)` where the first argument is hardcoded `[]`. `_deriveOpIncForFinancials` paths 1 (bank: totalRev - opEx - provisionForCreditLosses) and 2 (insurance: totalRev - costOfRevenue - SGA) both iterate over the first argument. With `[]`, both bank and insurance patterns produce zero non-null entries and always fall through to path 3 (margin × revenue). The variable `isHist` (the raw quoteSummary income statement rows) is a local variable inside `mapYahooToCanonical` and is not accessible at this call site inside `processOne`. The first invocation at line 570 correctly passes `isHist`, but the post-FTS second invocation at line 1792 cannot access it.
findings.mechanism: `isHist` is local to `mapYahooToCanonical`. The post-FTS fallback at line 1792 is in `processOne`. The canonical object does not preserve raw isHist rows. Bank/insurance derivation paths 1 and 2 are structurally unreachable in the post-FTS fallback.
findings.reproducer: Financial Services stock where Yahoo isHist has operatingExpenses+provisionForCreditLosses, FTS also returns all-null annualOpInc, and both QS and FTS paths have already run. The post-FTS fallback will use margin×revenue (path 3) instead of the more accurate bank pattern (path 1).
findings.impact: For banks with incomplete FTS data, opIncSource is reported as 'computed-margin' when it could be 'computed-bank'. Computed-margin applies a single TTM margin to all historical years, flattening year-by-year OpInc volatility.
findings.suggested_fix: Preserve `isHist` on the canonical object (e.g., `canonical._rawIsHist = isHist`) during mapping so the post-FTS fallback can access it. Or accept path 3 as the best available fallback but update the comment to document why paths 1/2 cannot fire here.

findings.title: mapFTSToQuarterly skips null-rev rows without null-placeholder — opIncQ/grossProfitQ misalignment
findings.description: mapFTSToQuarterly uses `if (rev == null) continue;` to skip rows with no totalRevenue, without pushing a null placeholder into opIncQ or grossProfitQ for the skipped row. This is the exact bug that was fixed in mapFTSToAnnual (F-DP-030/031 null-alignment fix). In mapFTSToAnnual, null-placeholder rows are pushed so that annualOpInc[i] aligns with annualRev[i] by fiscal year. mapFTSToQuarterly received no equivalent fix. If Yahoo's quarterly FTS data has a gap row (no totalRevenue) between two revenue-reporting rows, the resulting opIncQ array will be shorter than revenueQ, and index-based quarter pairing in downstream methods will be off by one.
findings.mechanism: `if (rev == null) continue` in mapFTSToQuarterly skips the entire row without pushing null into opIncQ or grossProfitQ. Downstream index-based access: revenueQ[i] and opIncQ[i] reference different quarters when any gap exists.
findings.reproducer: Ticker where Yahoo FTS quarterly financials contains a row with null totalRevenue but non-null operatingIncome — observable in restructuring or asset-sale quarters for financial-sector tickers.
findings.impact: Quarterly operating margin trend, acceleration-detection, and quarterly gross-margin calculations reference wrong quarters silently. False acceleration/deceleration signals.
findings.suggested_fix: Apply the same null-placeholder pattern as mapFTSToAnnual: when `rev == null`, push `null` into opIncQ and grossProfitQ (or better: push the row unconditionally with null values for missing fields).

findings.title: Yahoo schema-validation failures reclassified as not-found — silently marks valid stocks delisted
findings.description: The errClass switch at line 1930 reclassifies 'Failed Yahoo Schema validation' errors as errClass='not-found'. errClass=not-found triggers the delisted-flag write (lines 1934-1950): `existing.meta.delisted = true`. The intended use of this reclassification is to handle international tickers that genuinely don't exist in Yahoo's database — valid, but overly broad. When Yahoo adds a new response field that fails yahoo-finance2's strict schema validator (a recurring event — e.g. `impliedSharesOutstanding` was added in May 2026 per the Tag 211c comment in refresh-universe.js), the schema validation error would fire for EVERY ticker in the universe that returns the new field. Every affected ticker would be marked `delisted=true` in its snapshot. pull-yahoo's validation is suppressed globally (`logErrors:false`), so the only visible signal is a spike in the not-found failure count in the manifest — which would normally be attributed to genuine delistings.
findings.mechanism: Yahoo adds new response field → yahoo-finance2 strict validator throws 'Failed Yahoo Schema validation' for every ticker returning that field → errClass='not-found' → `meta.delisted=true` written to every affected snapshot.
findings.reproducer: Yahoo Finance API adds a new field to quoteSummary response for a module in MODULES list. yahoo-finance2 strict schema validation fires. All tickers that return the field are marked delisted.
findings.impact: Entire sections of the universe silently marked as delisted. Downstream pipeline treats them as removed from the universe on the next prune run. Recovery requires a git revert or manual intervention.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a counter for schema-validation failures and trigger a CRITICAL alert (or process.exit(1)) when schema-validation failures exceed 1% of attempted tickers. This distinguishes a Yahoo API schema change from individual ticker invalidations. Alternatively, reclassify schema-validation to a new errClass='schema-validation' that never writes the delisted flag.

findings.title: annualBalance override uses usable-row count but FTS/QS fiscal-year alignment diverges
findings.description: The annualBalance FTS override (line 1733-1735) fires when `newBalanceUsable > oldBalanceUsable`. When it fires, `canonical.annual.annualBalance` is replaced with `ftsBalance`. However, `annualRev` may NOT have been overridden (due to F-DP-004: length comparison fails). The result: `annualRev` comes from QS (starting at a certain fiscal year) and `annualBalance` comes from FTS (starting at a potentially different fiscal year or having different null-row positions). Methods that cross-index these arrays (ROIC uses annualRev[i] and annualBalance[i]; net-debt-EBITDA uses annualRev[i] and annualBalance[i].totalDebt) silently use mismatched fiscal years.
findings.mechanism: annualRev not overridden (F-DP-004 bug keeps QS null-filled array). annualBalance overridden with FTS data. QS income-statement years and FTS balance-sheet years may start from different fiscal periods. Index 0 in annualRev and index 0 in annualBalance are now different fiscal years.
findings.reproducer: Non-US ticker where QS incomeStatementHistory has 4 null-revenue rows (Nov-2024 schema drift) and FTS balance-sheet has 4 usable rows starting from a different year than QS.
findings.impact: ROIC, Altman Z-score, net-debt/EBITDA, Sloan accrual, Beneish M-score silently compute ratios from misaligned fiscal years. The numeric result looks plausible but is wrong.
findings.suggested_fix: Fix F-DP-004 first (non-null-count comparison for annualRev). If annualRev is also sourced from FTS, both arrays are year-aligned. Add an assertion: `if (canonical.annual.annualBalance.length !== canonical.annual.annualRev.length) _log('WARN', ...)` after the override.

findings.title: quarterly revenueQ FTS override uses length comparison — same null-density bug as annualRev
findings.description: `if (ftsQuarterly.revenueQ.length > canonical.timeseries.revenueQ.length)` at line 1769 uses a length comparison for quarterly revenue override. mapFTSToQuarterly does skip null-rev rows entirely (unlike mapFTSToAnnual which pushes placeholders), so FTS quarterly arrays will be shorter than QS if QS has null-rev gap rows. BUT: if QS's incomeStatementHistoryQuarterly also returns null-revenue rows (post-Nov-2024 schema drift), QS and FTS quarterly arrays can have the same length even if QS has all null values and FTS has non-null values. Length `4 > 4 = false` → override doesn't fire.
findings.mechanism: Same root cause as F-DP-004: length guard applied to quarterly revenue instead of non-null count.
findings.reproducer: Non-US ticker where Yahoo quoteSummary incomeStatementHistoryQuarterly returns sparse/null quarterly revenue, and FTS quarterly also returns the same count of rows (some with valid revenue).
findings.impact: Quarterly revenue timeseries stays null-filled. Acceleration-detection, quarterly gross-margin trend, and quarterly operating-leverage methods return computable:false.
findings.suggested_fix: Apply non-null count comparison to quarterly revenueQ override at line 1769, matching the annualOpInc pattern.

findings.title: refresh-universe screener-bucket loop has no retry on 429 — universe silently shrinks
findings.description: The `fetchScreener` function (line 125-136) catches all errors and returns `[]`. There is no distinction between 429 rate-limit (transient, should retry) and genuine data errors. With 325 sequential screener calls (25 regions × 13 buckets), Yahoo rate-limits are common — the exchange-screener path at lines 166-177 handles 429 with up to 3 retries and exponential backoff, but the predefined-screener path at lines 125-136 has no retry at all. A rate-limited bucket returns `[]` silently; the `console.warn` at line 133 logs it but does not retry. Contrast with fetchExchangePage which explicitly retries 429 responses. The log output at line 133 can be missed in noisy CI output.
findings.mechanism: fetchScreener's catch block returns `[]` on any error including 429. Yahoo rate-limits sequential calls at the pace of 300ms/call (13 screener × 25 regions = 325 calls). Any rate-limited call silently returns no tickers from that region/bucket combination.
findings.reproducer: Run refresh-universe.js when Yahoo is rate-limiting. WARN messages appear in logs but no retry occurs. Universe shrinks by however many tickers were in the rate-limited buckets.
findings.impact: Universe silently shrinks. New IPOs or mid-cap upgrades in affected regions/buckets are not discovered in this run. Since universe size changes are not alarmed on, the shrinkage can persist across multiple runs before detection.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a retry loop in `fetchScreener` for 429 responses, matching the pattern in `fetchExchangePage` (up to 3 attempts with exponential backoff).

findings.title: discovery/finnhub.js redirect handler does not drain response body — socket pool leak
findings.description: The `get()` function in finnhub.js handles 301/302 redirects without calling `res.resume()` before recursing. Node.js requires that HTTP response bodies be either consumed or explicitly drained; without this, the underlying TCP socket is held open and not returned to the connection pool. The identical bug was fixed in sec-tickers.js (Tag 229c-2) with `res.resume()`. Finnhub, wikipedia-indices, nasdaq-all, otc-markets, and nasdaq-api all have the same unfixed `get()` pattern. With 17 Finnhub exchange requests and potential CDN redirects, socket pool exhaustion can cause subsequent requests to hang waiting for a socket to be available.
findings.mechanism: 301/302 response body not drained in finnhub.js get(). TCP socket stays in CLOSE_WAIT state. After N redirects where N > the HTTPS agent's socket pool size, new requests block waiting for a free socket.
findings.reproducer: Finnhub CDN issues redirects for any exchange endpoint. Observable via `process._getActiveHandles().filter(h => h.constructor.name === 'TLSSocket').length` growing unboundedly.
findings.impact: Discovery requests hang or fail with ECONNRESET after socket pool exhaustion. Finnhub-sourced tickers are missed from the universe.
findings.suggested_fix: Add `res.resume();` in the 301/302 branch of all unfixed `get()` functions. Also add redirect chain depth limit and relative URL resolution from the Tag 229c-2 fix. Consider extracting a shared `lib/http-get.js` helper that all discovery modules use.

findings.title: discovery/wikipedia-indices.js, nasdaq-all.js, otc-markets.js: same socket-drain omission
findings.description: Same socket-drain omission as F-DP-012 in three additional discovery modules: wikipedia-indices.js (line 27), nasdaq-all.js (line 35), and otc-markets.js (line 40). All three have `get()` functions that recurse on 301/302 without calling `res.resume()`. The Tag 229c-2 fix (applied to sec-tickers.js) was not propagated to these modules. Combined, these 4 modules can leak 20+ sockets per run when CDN redirects occur.
findings.reproducer: Any CDN redirect on Wikipedia (article renaming), NASDAQ Trader FTP path changes, or OTC Markets CDN.
findings.impact: Socket pool exhaustion across discovery phase. Wikipedia (SP500/FTSE100/DAX constituents), NASDAQ Trader (~8k US tickers), and OTC Markets (~5k tickers) fail silently when their socket quota is consumed.
findings.suggested_fix: Apply Tag 229c-2 fix pattern to `get()` in wikipedia-indices.js, nasdaq-all.js, otc-markets.js, and nasdaq-api.js. Extract a shared lib/http-get.js helper.

findings.title: prune-watchlist.js deadReason uses only `meta.fetchedAt` — price-only-refreshed snapshots may be incorrectly pruned
findings.description: `deadReason` computes snapshot age from `snap.meta.fetchedAt`. In pull-yahoo.js, the price-only update path (`_priceOnlyUpdate`, line 1403) updates `existing.meta.asOf = newAsOf` but does NOT update `meta.fetchedAt`. After a stock has only received price-only refreshes for > maxAgeDays (default 60 days), its `meta.fetchedAt` will be 60+ days old. If the stock also has no revenue and no sector (pre-revenue biotech, or a stock where Yahoo returned no fundamentals), the condition `ageDays > maxAgeDays && !hasMcap && !hasRev && !hasSector` at line 93-99 can evaluate true. Since `hasMcap` checks `snap.marketCap.value > 0` and price-only updates DO maintain `marketCap.value`, most stocks are saved by `hasMcap=true`. But a stock with a valid marketCap but no revenue and no sector (common for pre-revenue biotechs) that has only received price-only refreshes for 60+ days will be pruned as 'no-active-quote' even though it has a fresh price.
findings.mechanism: `_priceOnlyUpdate` updates `meta.asOf` only. `deadReason` reads `meta.fetchedAt`. After 60+ days of price-only refreshes without a full pull, `fetchedAt` is stale. For stocks with `hasMcap=true` (most cases), the prune check saves them. For pre-revenue/no-sector stocks with valid mcap: `!hasMcap=false` prevents prune — actually `hasMcap=true` saves it, so the check `!hasMcap && !hasRev && !hasSector` requires ALL THREE to be absent/false. A stock with valid mcap would NOT be pruned. This finding is weaker than initially assessed.
findings.reproducer: Stock with marketCap=null (e.g., failed mcap from a previous full pull that left marketCap undefined), no revenue, no sector, and only price-only refreshes for 60+ days. `hasMcap=false`, `hasRev=false`, `hasSector=false` → pruned.
findings.impact: Valid stock that somehow has null marketCap in its snapshot but is being price-only refreshed (unusual: price-only requires marketCap to exist per line 1456) would be incorrectly pruned. Low likelihood but non-zero.
findings.suggested_fix: In `deadReason`, use `(snap.meta && (snap.meta.fetchedAt || snap.meta.asOf))` as the age source to correctly account for price-only-refreshed snapshots.

findings.title: FTS capitalExpenditure sign convention unverified post-Nov-2024 — could overstate FCF
findings.description: In `mapFTSToAnnual`, FCF is computed as `op + capex` with the comment 'capex is negative' (matching the quoteSummary convention where `capitalExpenditures` is always a negative number representing cash outflow). The FTS field name is `capitalExpenditure` (singular, different from QS's `capitalExpenditures` plural). Yahoo has historically changed sign conventions in FTS schema revisions. If the FTS `capitalExpenditure` field returns a POSITIVE value (magnitude of expenditure), then `op + capex` computes `OCF + |capex|` instead of `OCF - |capex|`, overstating FCF by 2×|capex|. For CAPEX-heavy companies (NVDA: $10B capex, AMZN: $60B) this is a catastrophic overstatement.
findings.mechanism: If Yahoo FTS `capitalExpenditure` sign flipped from negative to positive (as occurred with some Yahoo schema changes): FCF = op + positive_capex = OCF + capex_magnitude. This is systematically wrong for all FTS-sourced FCF values.
findings.reproducer: Inspect raw FTS cash-flow JSON response for NVDA or AMZN and check sign of `capitalExpenditure`. If > 0, FCF is massively overstated.
findings.impact: If sign is wrong: FCF overstated by 2×|capex| for every FTS-sourced ticker. FCF-yield, Rule-of-40, and reinvestment-rate methods all produce inflated values. Would cause many capex-heavy companies to score as having extremely high FCF margins.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a sign normalization in mapFTSToAnnual: `const capexNormalized = (capex != null && capex > 0) ? -capex : capex;` and use `capexNormalized` in the FCF computation. Also add a runtime sanity check: if `ftsAnnualCapex[0] > 0` for NVDA/AMZN, log a WARN.

findings.title: sortByStaleness reads only 300 bytes — `asOf` may be beyond offset 300 for long-name stocks
findings.description: The staleness probe reads 300 bytes from the start of each snapshot file and searches for the `asOf` or `fetchedAt` field. In compact JSON format (no indent), the meta object contains many fields before `fetchedAt`/`asOf`: ticker, name, sector, industry, region, exchangeName, reportingCurrency, tradingCurrency. For a stock with a long company name (e.g., 'STMicroelectronics N.V.' = 22 chars) and a long sector string, `fetchedAt` can appear beyond byte 300 in the file. When the regex misses, `age` defaults to 0 and the stock is treated as maximally stale, sorting to the front of the pull queue.
findings.mechanism: Buffer.alloc(300) truncates the JSON at 300 bytes. The `ageRegex` match fails. `age = 0` (sentinel for 'unknown/stale') sorts the stock first. Over a 15k-ticker universe, dozens of stocks may be affected, causing them to unnecessarily consume a full-pull slot on each run.
findings.reproducer: Stock with a company name >= 15 chars AND a long sector string (e.g., 'Information Technology' = 22 chars). In compact JSON: `{"identifier":{...},"meta":{"ticker":"STMN.SW","name":"STMicroelectronics N.V.","sector":"Information Technology",..."fetchedAt":"` — measure how many bytes to `fetchedAt`.
findings.impact: Cosmetic ordering issue only: affected stocks are pulled more frequently than needed. No data corruption. The `_getExistingSnapshotAge` function (line 1284) uses a 500-byte buffer and is used for the price-only vs full-pull decision, so no actual incorrect pull mode is selected.
findings.suggested_fix: Increase the sortByStaleness buffer from 300 to 1024 bytes. Alternatively, scan multiple chunks until the regex matches or the full file is read.

findings.title: refresh-universe sample log: `t.marketCap/1e9` produces '$0.0B' for null-mcap tickers from SEC/Wikipedia
findings.description: The sample-logging loop at line 448 calls `(t.marketCap/1e9).toFixed(1)` without null-checking. Discovery sources SEC EDGAR, Wikipedia, and NASDAQ Trader set `marketCap: null`. In JavaScript, `null / 1e9 === 0`, so `(null/1e9).toFixed(1) === '0.0'`. Every new ticker from these sources is logged as '$0.0B', which is misleading (appears to be a sub-$1M company rather than a newly-discovered ticker with unknown market cap).
findings.mechanism: `null / 1e9` in JavaScript coerces null to 0, yielding 0. `.toFixed(1)` on 0 yields '0.0'. Shown as '$0.0B' in the log for all null-mcap tickers.
findings.reproducer: Run refresh-universe.js when SEC EDGAR or Wikipedia contribute new tickers. The sample log shows '$0.0B' for every such entry.
findings.impact: Misleading log output only. No data corruption.
findings.suggested_fix: Replace the marketCap log expression with: `t.marketCap != null ? '$' + (t.marketCap/1e9).toFixed(1) + 'B' : 'mcap-unknown'`

findings.title: atomic-write _sleepSync uses Atomics.wait — blocks Node.js main thread on Windows rename retries
findings.description: `_sleepSync` uses `Atomics.wait(new Int32Array(sab), 0, 0, ms)` to implement a synchronous sleep. On the Node.js main thread, `Atomics.wait` is allowed (unlike browsers) but BLOCKS THE ENTIRE EVENT LOOP for the duration. The RENAME_RETRY_DELAYS_MS on Windows is `[10, 20, 50, 100, 200]` ms — worst case 380ms of cumulative event-loop blocking per file write. During a pull-yahoo run with 10 concurrent workers all hitting OneDrive-lock-induced EPERM on rename, up to 10 × 380ms = 3.8s of sequential event-loop blocking can stack (since each worker is async but writeFileAtomic is synchronous). During this blocking period, all 9 other workers' async callbacks are frozen — rate-limit sleep timers don't fire, network I/O callbacks don't process, the timeout timer for the current ticker's request doesn't advance.
findings.mechanism: Atomics.wait blocks the V8 main thread. writeFileAtomic is synchronous. In pull-yahoo, writeFileAtomic is called from processOne which is async, but the SYNC PORTION of processOne blocks the event loop while waiting for the rename. All concurrently scheduled async callbacks are deferred during this blocking period.
findings.reproducer: Windows development box with OneDrive sync client running. 10 concurrent workers each write a snapshot while OneDrive briefly holds a file handle → EPERM → 5-retry loop → 380ms blocked per worker. Cumulative: event loop frozen for ~1-3s.
findings.impact: Performance degradation on Windows development box. In CI (Ubuntu runner), RENAME_RETRY_DELAYS_MS=[] so this code path is never taken. No data corruption.
findings.suggested_fix: Consider making writeFileAtomic async (returning a Promise) so callers can await it without blocking the event loop. The rename retries would then use async setTimeout instead of synchronous Atomics.wait. This requires refactoring all callers to use `await writeFileAtomic(...)` but is architecturally cleaner.
